content: @bjorn3 for some reasons in the rust/Makefile I need the extension for the libmacros even if I don't have rust (for the no-clean-files ). Should I just move that under the ifdef CONFIG_RUST?
I have no clue. I hadn't actually used kbuild until two days ago.
Okay, will just move it, and ask Miguel about it in the pr

Hi @Finn Behrens, I read here [1] that you were working on supporting building the linux kernel with rust on macos hosts. I'm interested in that, so what is the status? do you have any branch I can look at?
[1]https://lore.kernel.org/all/20220927131518.30000-1-ojeda@kernel.org/

I had it working (I was using nix), the most problem was that I depended on another patch where an RFC got send, but nothing else ever happened, so I did not upstream my rust specific patch as the C side was missing some things as well. I have some things documented here as well. for the rust specific parts (like .dylib instead of .so) I don't have an up to date version, but should be fairly easy to update.

https://kloenk.eu/posts/build-linux-on-m1-macos/

Here are to commits, not sure how they currently apply
https://github.com/kloenk/linux/commits/rust-project_macos-dylib/
I have had the easiest time just building it in a virtualbox VM if you need to do this reliably
I tried to get it working in docker but that didn't ever work out for me
That being said I wasn't familiar with the work but it looks cool, I will have to give it a try :)

yeah vm works quite reliable as just linux. docker I usualy rather dislike, but if you fix the dependencies building directly on macos works quite nice, just those defines are a bit eh. there was the rfc from nick, but as never got merged/v2 you have to search and apply them yourself

@Finn Behrens, thanks for the links and the blog post. I will review them.

I have a working branch that I have been maintaining from time to time based on Nick Desaulniers RFC and his clang-for-linux/linux/macos branch.

I'm interested in pushing the changes upstream, so in case you are still interested I can cc you as well. But it would be great to have support for rust as well so that's why I was pinging you here.

yeah, currently moving so not that much time, but please ping me, would love to see Nicks and after that my changes upstream. Also happy to try it out/help updating my things to your current branch if that helps

at time time I only had to deal with the procmacros. not sure if anything changed since then or if that is still all that is needed, but rust should be not that much new where it needs special handling

that would be very nice. If you want to give it a try, I have this branch: https://gitlab.com/dkruces/linux-next/-/tree/macos/v6.10

I'm currently splitting Nick's patch into multiple ones starting with his subarch RFC.
probably wont have time to try myself soon. probably earliest middle of next month. but will see if I have the time. if not happy to review stuff
yes, no worries. I'll cc you when sending the RFC.

thanks for sending a patch, with moving I for now only skimed over it, looks quite nice, but let's see if Greg can be convinced that the kernel wants this :)
Hope to test is soonish as well

Greg needs a solid reason to accept the patches. But the feedback I got is actually to resolve the issues in Makefiles, so avoiding '__APPLE__' cases where we can.
On the other hand, the build team is asking to provide the missing headers via the package manager...

Let's see :)

Some of the feedback I got is already applied here: https://gitlab.com/dkruces/linux-next/-/tree/b4/macos-build-support?ref_type=heads

Although that branch works, it has the cross-build issue pointed by Nicolas.

But let me know if that works for you whenever you have the time. And if you have any feedback, please let me know. And of course, if you rebase your rust patches, I can apply them in case I send a v2.
@Daniel Gomez @Tamir Duberstein is seeking info on what is the latest and greatest in building the kernel with rust enabled on macOS. Did those patches merge upstream yet?

the rust ones quite sure not yet. tho IIRC it was mostly RA and clean that I did patch a while ago. Not sure how well the without C support is right now, pathes were on the list, but did not track them
I'd expect the Rust bits to be the least problematic, but I see errors very early in host tools: https://gist.github.com/tamird/2007fc246cee6dd2776cd7bd3fbfbc7d
hmm, yeah the first one looks like the UUID thing, I saw that in a patch but guess not applied yet. the other stuff, no clue. did not have that yet

Tamir Duberstein said:

I'd expect the Rust bits to be the least problematic, but I see errors very early in host tools: https://gist.github.com/tamird/2007fc246cee6dd2776cd7bd3fbfbc7d

This is supposed to be fixed in the user build environment.

HOSTCFLAGS="-D_UUID_T -D__GETHOSTUUID_H"

This is the last version of the “series”.

https://lore.kernel.org/all/20240925-macos-build-support-v3-1-233dda880e60@samsung.com/

Some patches were drop in favor of user build environment configuration like the one above.
How to configure the environment is provided in the series if anyone is interested

In a nutshell, after applying v3 + deps from Masahiro and installing bee-headers tap:

brew tap bee-headers/bee-headers
brew install bee-headers/bee-headers/bee-headers

source bee-init

make LLVM=1 defconfig
make LLVM=1 -j$(nproc)
Yeah, I'm running this now - seems to work! Thanks a lot.
To build with rust support i think we just need to rebase @Fiona Behrens’s patches. But I haven’t looked into that.

Tamir Duberstein said:

Yeah, I'm running this now - seems to work! Thanks a lot.

Thanks for trying it out!
Do you have a link to Fiona's patches? I'll try those out and resurrect them if needed.
they are quite quite old https://github.com/kloenk/linux/commits/rust-project_macos-dylib/
I'm having a bit of trouble even enabling the rust targets - are these instructions still correct? https://docs.kernel.org/rust/quick-start.html#configuration

tamird@Tamirs-MBP linux % grep RUST .config
CONFIG_RUSTC_VERSION=108100
CONFIG_RUST_IS_AVAILABLE=y
# CONFIG_RUST is not set

:thinking:
Ah, general setup first.

The very first error is

error[E0463]: can't find crate for `macros`
  --> rust/kernel/lib.rs:62:9
   |
62 | pub use macros;
   |         ^^^^^^ can't find crate

is that related to macos?
Ah, of course it is, that's the whole thing. Alright, that conflict wasn't bad and we're off to the races.
@Fiona Behrens would you want me to send this patch to the list? I cleaned it up just a bit.
if you do, please update the name. wanted to wait till the other one is in, and I then have time to update and send it
I can do that. The other one is the one daniel linked above?

yep, think that is the one. didn't have to much time to track it, therefore also no rebase yet.
(new name would be Fiona Behrens <me@kloenk.dev>)
Do I add my own S-O-B too?
afaik yes. not exactly sure if you change mine to co developed or something. don't have that process in mind right now
Got it. Somehow my compilation succeeded even without @Daniel Gomez's patch
Probably v3 is only needed when running allyesconfig. Also because the patch dependencies should have been merged in as well.
https://github.com/tamird/linux/commits/macos-build/
Have either of you folks seen any sensible tools for running the resulting kernel on a Mac?
qemu?
yeah, of course - but what do you use to build or supply the userland?
would propapbly use nixos to build userland. though not directly on the mac but some other vm/server because easier
right. if you're getting off the mac then you can also use virtme-ng

https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241008224810.84024-1-tamird@gmail.com/

hope I did it right
Can you hook a custom built kernel up to docker/podman? That would be kind of cool
It all boils down to the same problem of how to get a ready-made userland

Tamir Duberstein said:

Have either of you folks seen any sensible tools for running the resulting kernel on a Mac?

There are plenty of qemu wrappers available. One is virtme-ng you mentioned.

@Andreas Hindborg is maintaining one written in rust:

https://github.com/metaspace/run-kernel

I’m using https://github.com/SamsungDS/vmctl

Daniel Gomez said:

In a nutshell, after applying v3 + deps from Masahiro and installing bee-headers tap:

brew tap bee-headers/bee-headers
brew install bee-headers/bee-headers/bee-headers

source bee-init

make LLVM=1 defconfig
make LLVM=1 -j$(nproc)

This should probably be in the documentation?

Tamir Duberstein said:

It all boils down to the same problem of how to get a ready-made userland

I think most of the tools expect a Linux host. run-kernel might work if you disable the file system sharing feature. It will bring up a debian userland and run your kernel with that through qemu.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Daniel Gomez said:

In a nutshell, after applying v3 + deps from Masahiro and installing bee-headers tap:

brew tap bee-headers/bee-headers
brew install bee-headers/bee-headers/bee-headers

source bee-init

make LLVM=1 defconfig
make LLVM=1 -j$(nproc)

This should probably be in the documentation?

Documentation patch got NACKed unfortunately. So, I moved it here: https://github.com/bee-headers/homebrew-bee-headers

Daniel Gomez said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Daniel Gomez said:

In a nutshell, after applying v3 + deps from Masahiro and installing bee-headers tap:

brew tap bee-headers/bee-headers
brew install bee-headers/bee-headers/bee-headers

source bee-init

make LLVM=1 defconfig
make LLVM=1 -j$(nproc)

This should probably be in the documentation?

Documentation patch got NACKed unfortunately. So, I moved it here: https://github.com/bee-headers/homebrew-bee-headers

I don’t understand. Was there some kind of problem with the documentation? Or did they just not want to document how to build on macOS?
The UUID compiler setting seems important as well

Not exactly. Building the kernel in macOS means building in a “foreign” host. So, it is expected by the user to provide the missing dependencies as well as the environment. They don’t want to deal with a full integration in the build system but it’s okay to modify it and make it “portable” when required. That’s the summary of the feedback in the v1 and v2 series.

So, almost all dependencies can be provided using homebrew, except for byteswap, endian and elf headers. I created a bee-headers project for reference on how to do that. And/Or just simply use it.

It provides the environment as well as the headers.

Andreas Hindborg said:

The UUID compiler setting seems important as well

It is. Is should be fixed in the user's build environment.
source bee-init does that.

Daniel Gomez said:

Not exactly. Building the kernel in macOS means building in a “foreign” host. So, it is expected by the user to provide the missing dependencies as well as the environment. They don’t want to deal with a full integration in the build system but it’s okay to modify it and make it “portable” when required. That’s the summary of the feedback in the v1 and v2 series.

So, almost all dependencies can be provided using homebrew, except for byteswap, endian and elf headers. I created a bee-headers project for reference on how to do that. And/Or just simply use it.

It provides the environment as well as the headers.

Very cool.

For those wanting to follow along, I resent the rust patch in https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241016-b4-dylib-host-macos-v6-1-bbeab5029199@gmail.com/ with a better explanation, now that I properly understand what's going on in the rust compiler :upside_down:

EDIT: wrong link

Sort of related, is anyone interested in getting the kernel to not have files that differ only by case, so you can clone it on a default Mac filesystem? (And would the relevant maintainers be open to treating this as something worth fixing?) The set of files is pretty small, a handful in netlink and one memory model test case.
I had to reinstall my Mac to a case sensitive filesystem :woozy_face:
You can just create a partition in the same system without reinstalling. Not a big deal I think.

Geoffrey Thomas said:

something worth fixing?) The set of files is pretty small, a handful in netlink and one memory model test case.

I’m not sure if it’s worth it? Are there more Linux projects with the same issue that require this?
Rather Apple should fix their file system :joy:
If anyone has a few cycles to review my fix for build on mac I'd be grateful. It's been sitting for a couple of months.

Tamir Duberstein said:

If anyone has a few cycles to review my fix for build on mac I'd be grateful. It's been sitting for a couple of months.

Thanks for doing this Tamir. I tested this in Friday with the rnull driver built-in and as module. I’ll send a tag tomorrow :)

Thank you Daniel. I rebased and sent v7 with your tag.

@Miguel Ojeda is there anyone else in particular whose signoff is necessary to proceed with this? You mentioned kbuild in the past but there hasn't been engagement from those maintainers.

It doesn't touch anything major, so I think I can pick it without an explicit Acked-by, especially since other explicit macOS bits got into mainline already from my understanding in 6.12.

Thanks Daniel for double-checking on the macOS side, appreciated!

I may add a small comment in a couple places to mention it is needed for systems like macOS to avoid forgetting about it.
Makes sense to me. Thanks Miguel! This has been an annoying papercut :)
FYI, the Rust modversion support patches from Sami will “break” macOS support. The new tool won’t build as it’s just missing some dependencies on macOS (libdw-dev and others).
Interesting. Do you know why libdw was chosen over e.g. libdwarf?
Im not aware of the reasoning. Is there any benefit of using one over the other?
Here the patches in case you want to review them: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-modules/Z0YTrwR67-5T86E6@bombadil.infradead.org/T/#m8eadc20b7c6a43206b8e8200e979f2469c79e070
Yeah I found them. libdwarf is cross-platform
Seems like libdw is used all over the place
The idea here is to merge the tool and at some point deprecate the c-only variant, which at the moment has no official maintainer. And use this new one for both c and rust.
Interesting I wonder then if we could use libdwarf ...?
People on the internet don't have good things to say about libdwarf :(

Are both libraries API equivalent? Or would it require major rewrite of the tool?

Tamir Duberstein said:

Interesting I wonder then if we could use libdwarf ...?
I really don't know
But it seems like libdw is the newer of the two, so this sounds like a non-starter. I think we just want to make sure things degrade as gracefully as possible in the absence of libdw
I recently used blazesym for eBPF and kernel stack trace symbol print. Blazesym is written in rust
Im not sure if it can be used for the same purposed though.

Tamir Duberstein said:

But it seems like libdw is the newer of the two, so this sounds like a non-starter. I think we just want to make sure things degrade as gracefully as possible in the absence of libdw

Maybe they already had the tool and knowledge and they preferred going with it.
blazesym is different, right? my understanding is that this series is about generating ABI information

Daniel Gomez said:

FYI, the Rust modversion support patches from Sami will “break” macOS support. The new tool won’t build as it’s just missing some dependencies on macOS (libdw-dev and others).

Thanks for letting me know -- will it break only if modversions is enabled, or in all cases?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Daniel Gomez said:

FYI, the Rust modversion support patches from Sami will “break” macOS support. The new tool won’t build as it’s just missing some dependencies on macOS (libdw-dev and others).

Thanks for letting me know -- will it break only if modversions is enabled, or in all cases?

I’m not sure if my use of “break” is the right verb here since we don’t have support for modversions in rust as of now.

And yes, it will only “break” when modversions is enabled and this new tool is chosen.
We aim to support only one and this is likely to be the default at some point.

Tamir Duberstein said:

blazesym is different, right? my understanding is that this series is about generating ABI information

Yes. I’m not aware of the differences between libraries. Just reading from the docs:

“blazesym supports a variety of formats, such as DWARF, ELF, Breakpad, and Gsym (see below for an up-to-date list).”

But anyway the target os is Linux and macOS has only limited support.

We should instead use the one you are suggesting for cross platform support.
Like I said I'm not sure libdwarf is a viable alternative. People on the internet claim it is slow, for instance
Yes, I was reading about that.

Miguel Ojeda said:

It doesn't touch anything major, so I think I can pick it without an explicit Acked-by, especially since other explicit macOS bits got into mainline already from my understanding in 6.12.

Thanks Daniel for double-checking on the macOS side, appreciated!

I may add a small comment in a couple places to mention it is needed for systems like macOS to avoid forgetting about it.

Is this still the plan of record?
Happy new year folks. @Miguel Ojeda how can I help nudge this?
Hey @Daniel Gomez , any interest in resending https://lore.kernel.org/all/20240925-macos-build-support-v3-1-233dda880e60@samsung.com/?

Tamir Duberstein said:

Hey Daniel Gomez , any interest in resending https://lore.kernel.org/all/20240925-macos-build-support-v3-1-233dda880e60@samsung.com/?

Not for now. It’s a fix for an intel driver when using allyesconfig with arm64. But it would be required when enabling x86_64 support.
Bummer. I ask because I hit it when testing with allmodconfig on arm64 yesterday.
I got some early feedback and those were addressed. So, it should be almost ready. I was not pushing on this front as there are other problems in macOS that prevent me from using it regularly.
What are the other problems? I've been using it for a while.
eBPF support was a blocker for me (CONFIG_DEBUG_INFO_BTF)

Daniel Gomez said:

eBPF support was a blocker for me (CONFIG_DEBUG_INFO_BTF)

Ack. Consider this a gentle request for a resend, then. :)
Assuming the folks following here care about building on macOS: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20250210-macros-section-v2-1-3bb9ff44b969@gmail.com/ could use a reviewer and/or a tester. :folded_hands:
Would #[cfg(not(test))] also work to always disable the section when doing userspace tests? Kunit tests shouldn't set cfg(test), but I'm not sure if the way we handle doctests does set cfg(test).
doctests are KUnit tests, aren't they? I think not(test) would probably work, but I was looking for the narrowest solution
Right, regular doctests are KUnit tests. The proc macro doctests however are not KUnit tests I think. Otherwise they wouldn't get compiled for Mach-O.

Ah, I see what you're saying now. I wouldn't expect how we compile KUnit tests to set cfg(test), but I'd also wager that we wouldn't detect this error if they did (now or in the future).

But as you say, the (proc macro) doctests don't set cfg(test), so using cfg(not(test)) doesn't solve the problem on macOS (because the failure happens when building the proc macro doctest).
#[cfg(not(doctest))] should work. Rustdoc sets cfg(doctest) for doc tests.

Ah, but because this check is in the body of the macro (rather than on its definition) it might remove test coverage of the section name functionality from KUnit tests, depending on how they are compiled (again, now or in the future).
cfg(doctest) is not set for KUnit based doctests afaik, only for doctests that run on the host.
It isn't difficult to imagine that changing in the future, though. We write those tests as doctests, it stands to reason we might want the usual doctest conditional compilation for them.
We could pass --cfg test_on_host or something like that to rustdoc when running doctests on the host.
Can I ask why these alternatives are preferable to just not using a section name that is not permitted on the target?

There may be other targets where it doesn't work and the .modinfo section only makes sense when linking as external module or linking into the kernel itself. There is no need for it when running on the host.
That's a fair point. But then the cfg should just mention "host", not necessarily test. Right?
That would also work. For consistency in that case it should probably also be passed when compiling proc-macros as well as tools written in rust.

I'll spin that off into another thread in the channel since we'll want a larger audience. But first, interestingly cfg_attr(not(doctest), ... also did not prevent the build failure. Any idea why that would be?
Weird. Unfortunately I don't have a clue why that wouldn't work.
Thinking about this again, I don't think doing a not(host) thing makes sense here since you wouldn't use this macro in host code.

The proc macro and doctests for the proc macro would get compiled with --cfg host. When the proc macro is used outside of a doctest of the proc macro itself (that is the output gets compiled into the kernel), you wouldn't pass --cfg host. The #[cfg] of the crate that uses the proc macro is used for #[cfg] that get produced by proc macros.

Tamir Duberstein said:

Assuming the folks following here care about building on macOS: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20250210-macros-section-v2-1-3bb9ff44b969@gmail.com/ could use a reviewer and/or a tester. :folded_hands:

@Tamir Duberstein are you planning to send a v3? Not sure if there’s going to be a change as per discussion above.

@Daniel Gomez I'm still looking for some more opinions. @bjorn3's last reply explains why using --cfg host would solve the problem but not why it is morally the correct way to solve it. IMO semantically it's the wrong solution because this kind of macro wouldn't be used on host in the first place. Using cfg_attr(doctest, ...) is starting to feel right, but as I mentioned above: doesn't work properly.
